refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across representative worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is refined to be measurable, time-bound, and scenario-aware. It explicitly ties the functional requirement to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and acknowledges that energy efficiency is a system-level property dependent on operator behavior, vehicle design, and operational context. The refinement separates the capability (enable achievement) from the outcome (measured performance), allowing decomposition into sub-requirements addressing sensing, control, feedback, and operational guidance.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton across a defined evaluation period covering multiple representative worksite scenarios (e.g., load types, terrain, operator skill levels). Compare measured value against E_target. Verify that the NGHE solution's design, telemetry, and operational support mechanisms contribute measurably to achieving the target.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The NGHE solution shall acquire and report real-time energy consumption data (power draw, fuel consumption, or equivalent) with sufficient accuracy and latency to enable energy-aware decision-making.
FR-EE-002: The NGHE solution shall compute and display energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton, efficiency trends) to the operator and tele-operator in real-time.

FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall provide operational guidance (e.g., optimal speed, load distribution, cycle recommendations) to minimize energy consumption while maintaining safety and productivity.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy performance data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Implement a closed-loop energy monitoring and advisory system that continuously acquires vehicle energy telemetry, computes efficiency metrics in real-time, and provides operator guidance via HMI and tele-operator interface.

DP-EE-B: Integrate energy-efficient vehicle subsystems (optimized drivetrain, regenerative braking, load-aware transmission) with a passive telemetry logging system; rely on post-mission analysis and operator experience for efficiency optimization.

DP-EE-C: Implement a hybrid approach: active real-time energy monitoring and feedback (DP-EE-A) combined with vehicle-level efficiency optimization (DP-EE-B) and predictive energy modeling to guide mission planning.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-C: Implement a hybrid energy efficiency architecture comprising (1) real-time energy telemetry acquisition and HMI feedback to the operator and tele-operator, (2) vehicle-level efficiency optimizations (drivetrain, braking, transmission), and (3) predictive energy modeling integrated into mission planning and operational guidance systems. This approach addresses both immediate operator awareness and systemic vehicle efficiency, reducing coupling between operator behavior and vehicle design.

potential_sources: Coupling between real-time feedback (HMI) and vehicle control systems: mitigated by clear interface boundaries (Telemetry in, Tasking out).
Coupling between energy modeling and mission planning: managed via predictive data flow (Telemetry -> modeling -> Tasking guidance).
Coupling between tele-operator guidance and vehicle autonomy: addressed by explicit SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces.
coupling_check.notes: DP-EE-C is less coupled than DP-EE-A (which relies solely on operator response) and more effective than DP-EE-B (which lacks real-time feedback). The hybrid approach distributes responsibility across vehicle design, real-time monitoring, and operational guidance, reducing single-point failure modes and enabling continuous improvement.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle subsystems (drivetrain, braking, transmission) can be optimized for energy efficiency without compromising safety or productivity. Risk: Over-optimization for efficiency may reduce responsiveness or load capacity; mitigation via safety-critical testing.

Assumption: Predictive energy modeling is sufficiently accurate across diverse worksite scenarios. Risk: Model inaccuracy leads to poor guidance; mitigation via continuous model refinement using logged telemetry.

Assumption: Operator and tele-operator will act on energy efficiency guidance. Risk: Guidance ignored if perceived as conflicting with productivity or safety; mitigation via HMI design and stakeholder training.

Assumption: Real-time telemetry acquisition and processing do not introduce latency that degrades vehicle responsiveness. Risk: Feedback loop instability; mitigation via latency budgeting and control system validation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-EE-C is sound but should be refined to explicitly specify: (1) telemetry acquisition frequency and accuracy requirements, (2) energy metric computation latency budget, (3) guidance algorithm logic (e.g., speed optimization, load balancing), and (4) integration points with vehicle control and mission planning systems. These refinements will clarify DP-IM mapping and reduce implementation ambiguity.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system integration module responsible for coordinating energy efficiency across all subsystems. It owns the energy monitoring architecture, real-time feedback mechanisms, predictive modeling, and operational guidance integration. It interfaces with Vehicle (for telemetry and control), Teleoperation (for operator/tele-operator guidance), and Safety Services (for safety-critical energy constraints).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Real-time energy consumption data (power draw, fuel consumption, vehicle state) from Vehicle subsystems. Used to compute energy_per_ton and drive real-time feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operational guidance (speed recommendations, load distribution, cycle optimization) sent to operator and tele-operator via Teleoperation interface. Derived from energy modeling and efficiency analysis.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services. Energy optimization must not violate safety limits (e.g., braking distance, stability margins). Alerts may trigger energy guidance adjustments.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for telemetry transmission (in), guidance dissemination (out), and coordination with tele-operator and Safety Services.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of system-level energy efficiency architecture. It integrates telemetry, modeling, feedback, and guidance. Minimal diffusion: energy monitoring logic is centralized; vehicle-level optimizations are owned by Vehicle module but coordinated by NGHE Solution.

Vehicle owns drivetrain, braking, and transmission optimizations (DP-EE-C component 2). However, real-time monitoring, feedback, and guidance (DP-EE-C components 1 and 3) are system-level concerns better owned by NGHE Solution. High diffusion if Vehicle attempts to own energy modeling and operator guidance.

Teleoperation owns HMI and operator/tele-operator interfaces for guidance presentation. However, energy modeling and metric computation are system-level; diffusion risk if Teleoperation attempts to own energy logic. Clear separation: Teleoperation displays guidance; NGHE Solution computes it.

Safety Services owns safety constraints and alerts. Energy efficiency must respect safety limits, but Safety Services should not own energy optimization logic. High diffusion risk if Safety Services attempts to enforce energy targets; this is a performance KPI, not a safety requirement.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the optimal owner because it: (1) integrates telemetry from Vehicle, (2) owns energy modeling and metric computation, (3) coordinates guidance via Teleoperation, and (4) respects safety constraints from Safety Services. This placement minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns: Vehicle handles mechanical efficiency, NGHE Solution handles system-level monitoring and guidance, Teleoperation handles HMI, Safety Services handles safety constraints.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution becomes a 'god module' if it attempts to own too much logic. Mitigation: Clearly delineate energy monitoring (NGHE Solution), vehicle optimization (Vehicle), HMI (Teleoperation), and safety (Safety Services). Use well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link).

Risk: Vehicle module may resist providing detailed telemetry if it perceives energy monitoring as intrusive. Mitigation: Establish Telemetry interface contract early; ensure Vehicle benefits from energy data (e.g., diagnostics, predictive maintenance).

Risk: Tele-operator may ignore or misinterpret energy guidance if not well-integrated into Teleoperation HMI. Mitigation: Involve Teleoperation module early in guidance design; ensure guidance is actionable and non-conflicting with safety/productivity.

Risk: Safety Services may impose energy constraints that conflict with efficiency targets. Mitigation: Establish clear priority: safety > efficiency. Use SafetyAlert interface to communicate constraints; design energy guidance to respect them.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: NGHE Solution ownership is sound. Recommended refinements: (1) Define Telemetry interface schema (energy fields, sampling rate, latency), (2) Define Tasking interface for energy guidance (format, priority, conflict resolution), (3) Establish SafetyAlert protocol for safety-energy trade-offs, (4) Allocate energy modeling and metric computation to specific sub-modules within NGHE Solution (e.g., Energy Monitor, Efficiency Analyzer, Guidance Engine) to avoid monolithic design.

fr_dp_gate.reason: FR-EE (refined) and DP-EE-C are well-aligned. The refined FR specifies measurable energy efficiency targets and acknowledges system-level dependencies. DP-EE-C addresses all sub-FRs: (1) real-time telemetry (FR-EE-001), (2) metric computation and display (FR-EE-002), (3) operational guidance (FR-EE-003), and (4) data logging (FR-EE-004). The hybrid approach (real-time feedback + vehicle optimization + predictive modeling) is neither over-constrained nor under-specified. Coupling analysis passes: interfaces are clear, and responsibility is distributed appropriately.

diffusion_risks: Moderate diffusion risk: Energy monitoring logic could be split across NGHE Solution, Vehicle, and Teleoperation if interfaces are not clearly defined. Mitigation: Establish Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interface contracts before implementation.

Moderate diffusion risk: Predictive energy modeling could be duplicated in mission planning and real-time guidance if ownership is ambiguous. Mitigation: Centralize modeling in NGHE Solution; expose via well-defined APIs.
Low diffusion risk: Vehicle-level optimizations (drivetrain, braking) are naturally owned by Vehicle module; NGHE Solution coordinates via Telemetry and SafetyAlert.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the clear owner of system-level energy efficiency architecture. It integrates telemetry, modeling, feedback, and guidance. Vehicle owns mechanical optimizations. Teleoperation owns HMI. Safety Services owns safety constraints. Interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert inout, Network Link inout). Containment score for NGHE Solution is 95; diffusion is minimal if interfaces are enforced.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The refined FR is measurable (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and verifiable via field testing. NGHE Solution can log telemetry and compute metrics; verification requires controlled evaluation period and representative scenarios. Sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-004) are independently verifiable: telemetry accuracy, metric computation, guidance quality, and data logging can be tested in isolation and integration.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR-EE is allocated to NGHE Solution, which owns energy monitoring, modeling, and guidance. Sub-FRs are allocated to sub-modules within NGHE Solution (Energy Monitor, Efficiency Analyzer, Guidance Engine) and coordinated with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via interfaces. Ownership boundaries are explicit; no ambiguity.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-EE-C to specify telemetry acquisition frequency, energy metric computation latency, and guidance algorithm logic. This will clarify IM sub-module responsibilities.

Define Telemetry interface schema (energy fields, units, sampling rate, accuracy requirements) before implementation. This will reduce diffusion risk and enable Vehicle module to design telemetry acquisition accordingly.
Establish SafetyAlert protocol for safety-energy trade-offs. Define how Safety Services communicates constraints to NGHE Solution and how NGHE Solution adjusts guidance in response.

Plan for continuous model refinement: NGHE Solution should log telemetry and guidance outcomes to improve predictive energy modeling over time. This requires data pipeline design and feedback loop closure.

decision.justification: FR-EE (refined) is well-defined and measurable. DP-EE-C is sound and addresses all sub-FRs. NGHE Solution is the clear owner with minimal diffusion risk. However, DP-EE-C is not yet atomic; it comprises three major components (real-time monitoring, vehicle optimization, predictive modeling) that require further decomposition. Sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-004) are ready for the next decomposition level. Additionally, interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) must be refined before implementation to minimize diffusion and ensure integration clarity. Proceeding to the next level will decompose sub-FRs into atomic requirements and design parameters, and will establish interface specifications.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR is clear and verifiable. DP-EE-C is a sound hybrid approach that balances real-time feedback, vehicle efficiency, and predictive guidance. NGHE Solution ownership is justified by high containment score (95) and minimal diffusion. However, DP-EE-C is composite and requires further decomposition. Sub-FRs are well-scoped and ready for detailed design. Interface contracts are critical to success; they must be refined in parallel with sub-FR decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The NGHE solution shall acquire and report real-time energy consumption data (power draw, fuel consumption, or equivalent) with sufficient accuracy and latency to enable energy-aware decision-making.
FR-EE-002: The NGHE solution shall compute and display energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton, efficiency trends) to the operator and tele-operator in real-time.

FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall provide operational guidance (e.g., optimal speed, load distribution, cycle recommendations) to minimize energy consumption while maintaining safety and productivity.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy performance data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystems can be optimized for energy efficiency without compromising safety or productivity.
Predictive energy modeling is sufficiently accurate across diverse worksite scenarios.
Operator and tele-operator will act on energy efficiency guidance.
Real-time telemetry acquisition and processing do not introduce latency that degrades vehicle responsiveness.
Energy efficiency is a system-level property dependent on vehicle design, operator behavior, and operational context.
Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces can be clearly defined and enforced.
NGHE Solution can integrate energy monitoring, modeling, and guidance without becoming a monolithic 'god module'.
open_risks: Model inaccuracy: Predictive energy modeling may not generalize across diverse worksite scenarios. Mitigation: Continuous model refinement using logged telemetry.
Operator compliance: Operators may ignore energy guidance if perceived as conflicting with productivity or safety. Mitigation: HMI design, stakeholder training, and incentive alignment.

Interface ambiguity: Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces must be clearly specified before implementation. Risk: Diffusion and integration failures. Mitigation: Establish interface contracts early.
Safety-efficiency trade-offs: Energy optimization may conflict with safety constraints. Mitigation: Establish clear priority (safety > efficiency) and SafetyAlert protocol.
Latency: Real-time telemetry and feedback may introduce latency that degrades vehicle responsiveness. Mitigation: Latency budgeting and control system validation.

High safety criticality: Energy efficiency is a performance KPI, but the system operates in a safety-critical domain (heavy equipment). Risk: Energy optimization inadvertently compromises safety. Mitigation: Safety-critical testing and SafetyAlert integration.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: energy fields, units, sampling rate, accuracy requirements, latency budget.
Energy metric computation specification: energy_per_ton formula, metric accuracy, computation latency, display format.
Operational guidance specification: guidance types (speed, load, cycle), algorithm logic, conflict resolution with safety/productivity.
Data logging specification: telemetry fields, logging frequency, storage capacity, post-evaluation analysis requirements.
Predictive energy modeling specification: model inputs, outputs, accuracy targets, refinement process.
SafetyAlert protocol: safety constraint types, alert format, NGHE Solution response logic.
Vehicle-level optimization specification: drivetrain, braking, transmission efficiency improvements, integration with NGHE Solution.
HMI design for energy guidance: display format, operator interaction, tele-operator integration.
Field evaluation plan: evaluation period, representative scenarios, energy_per_ton measurement methodology, acceptance criteria.
